startScreenOptions.title: AN32.1-AN32.2 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 2 embedded questions for AN32.1-AN32.2.

interactions.label: A midline cystic swelling below the hyoid bone moves with both swallowing and to

params.question: A midline cystic swelling below the hyoid bone moves with both swallowing and tongue protrusion. Which embryological connection best explains this dual movement?

answers.text: Attachment to the strap muscles and investing layer of deep cervical fascia

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Attachment to strap muscles could explain movement with swallowing, but not with tongue protrusion. The dual movement requires a structure connecting to both the tongue and the thyroid.

answers.text: A persistent thyroglossal tract linking the foramen caecum to the thyroid gland

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The thyroglossal tract runs from the foramen caecum at the tongue base to the thyroid. A cyst arising from this tract retains connections to both structures, explaining movement with both swallowing and tongue protrusion.

answers.text: A branchial cleft remnant connected to the pharyngeal wall

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Branchial cleft remnants produce lateral neck swellings, typically along the anterior border of sternocleidomastoid — not midline cystic swellings below the hyoid.

answers.text: Fibrous adhesions between the cyst wall and the hyoid bone

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Hyoid adhesions alone might explain movement with swallowing, but they cannot explain movement with tongue protrusion. The key is the thyroglossal tract's dual connection to both the tongue base and the thyroid.

overallFeedback.feedback: The thyroglossal tract connects the foramen caecum at the tongue base to the thyroid gland. A cyst on this tract moves with swallowing (thyroid connection) and with tongue protrusion (tongue base connection).

Well done! The thyroglossal tract is the embryological path of thyroid descent from the foramen caecum to its pretracheal position, and its persistence explains the cyst's characteristic dual movement.

interactions.label: Stimulation of the carotid sinus during a neck examination causes reflex bradyca

params.question: Stimulation of the carotid sinus during a neck examination causes reflex bradycardia and a fall in blood pressure. This response occurs because the carotid sinus contains:

answers.text: Chemoreceptors that detect low oxygen and rising carbon dioxide

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Chemoreceptors are found in the carotid body, not the carotid sinus. The carotid body detects low oxygen and rising carbon dioxide, triggering increased respiratory drive — a different reflex entirely.

answers.text: Baroreceptors that detect changes in arterial blood pressure

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The carotid sinus is a slight dilatation at the origin of the internal carotid artery. Its baroreceptors sense arterial wall stretch and trigger a vagal reflex causing bradycardia and vasodilation.

answers.text: Proprioceptors that monitor neck vessel wall stretch during movement

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Proprioceptors monitor muscle and joint position, not arterial pressure. The specialised receptors in the carotid sinus are baroreceptors — pressure sensors that regulate cardiovascular reflexes.

answers.text: Nociceptors that trigger a protective vagal response to compression

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The carotid sinus reflex is a physiological blood pressure homeostatic mechanism mediated by baroreceptors, not a pain-triggered protective response via nociceptors.

overallFeedback.feedback: The carotid sinus contains baroreceptors that detect arterial wall stretch. Their stimulation triggers vagal-mediated bradycardia and hypotension. The nearby carotid body, by contrast, contains chemoreceptors for oxygen and carbon dioxide sensing.

Excellent! Remember that the carotid sinus (baroreceptors, pressure sensing) and the carotid body (chemoreceptors, gas sensing) are distinct structures at the carotid bifurcation with different clinical significance.
